strict_win_deltas.delta: P1 keeps the file's single global `app.use(express.json())` pattern and just adds a `verify` callback to capture `req.rawBody`, while P2 splits parsing into a webhook-only `express.raw()` middleware plus a relocated `app.use(express.json())` placed after the `/webhook` route, fragmenting the body-parsing strategy across the file.

P1 reads the secret with `.trim()` (`fs.readFileSync(...).trim()`), avoiding HMAC mismatches from a trailing newline in `data/webhook-secret.txt`, whereas P2's `fs.readFileSync(...)` has no `.trim()`, so a trailing newline in the secret file (a common artifact of how such files get created) would make every legitimately-signed request fail verification in production.

P2 manually runs `JSON.parse(req.rawBody.toString('utf8'))` inside a try/catch after `hasValidWebhookSignature(req)`, so even a malformed/non-JSON body still gets the signature checked first and yields the spec's `{error:'invalid_body'}`, whereas P1's global `express.json()` parses (and can throw) before the `/webhook` handler ever runs, letting Express's default error handler short-circuit past the signature check the task says 'still runs first'.

P2 factors logic into named helpers `hasValidWebhookSignature(req)` and `isValidWebhookBody(body)`, whereas P1 inlines all signature and shape-validation logic directly inside the `app.post('/webhook', ...)` callback, making P2's route handler more readable and its checks independently reusable/testable.
